steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims people have mixed feelings about cultured meat — both positive and negative attitudes — that may slow worldwide adoption. The evidence must show both sides of that tension in one passage.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — “Many consumers who were surveyed believed that cultured meat would be good for the environment if widely available, but few of those same consumers were willing to try eating cultured meat themselves.”: "Good for the environment" is the positive; "few were willing to try" is the negative. Both attitudes are present and in direct conflict — exactly the mixed feelings the student claims. ✓
  • Choice B — “The flavor of meat from livestock differs across species (from pig to chicken to cow), and is also influenced by farming conditions and the breeds and genders of animals.”: Describes conventional meat variety; says nothing about attitudes toward cultured meat. ✗
  • Choice C — “Advocates of lab-grown meat claim that it’s safer than conventional meat because it doesn’t come from animals raised in spaces that make them more vulnerable to illness.”: One-sided positive view from advocates only; no negative attitude, no mixed feelings. ✗
  • Choice D — “Consumers tend to believe that using less packaging when selling meat products in stores would have a significant effect on the environment.”: About packaging attitudes, not about cultured meat at all. Irrelevant. ✗
A is correct.
